(23) Learning Hexo from Scratch - SEO: Adding Your 404 Page
This post covers why the 404 page also affects SEO, with an implementation walkthrough.
What is 404? Can you eat it?
404 means a page doesn't exist or has an error. If a user accidentally lands on a non-existent page, they'll see this 404 page. On my site, if you click

you'll hit the 404 page, which redirects back to home after about five seconds. The main purpose of a 404 page is to improve user experience — it usually has a logo or a "back to home" button, so users don't just bounce immediately.
The reason this matters is that Google's search engine factors in whether you have a useful 404 page. Details in Google's
Search Engine Optimization (SEO) Starter Guide — Create useful 404 pages
If you're using GitHub as your blog host like me, I'd especially recommend setting up a custom 404, because Google doesn't accept GitHub's default 404 page.
There are several ways to build a 404 page. One is to create a new page (see
(10) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Adding New Pages
) and write it yourself. Keep these Google SEO recommendations in mind:

Avoid:
Letting your 404 pages be indexed in search engines (make sure that your web server is configured to give a 404 HTTP status code or — in the case of JavaScript-based sites — include the noindex robots meta tag).
Blocking 404 pages from being crawled through the robots.txt file.
Providing only a vague message like "Not found", "404", or no 404 page at all.
Using a design for your 404 pages that isn't consistent with the rest of your site.
We'll cover robots.txt later. For now, create a new 404 page with:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
to get a working 404 page with redirect:
language-md shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Sorry, the page you're looking for doesn't exist.
You'll be sent back to the home page in about <
If you're in a hurry to read articles, you can
// remember to change this to your own URL
Remember to update the redirect URL to your own.
is critical — without it, the 404 page won't show up correctly.
The other way is to write your own
That's the 404 page walkthrough. With this, you should have a better grip on Hexo. See you in the next one.
